text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male with known stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, microsatellite stable) with bilobar hepatic metastases. Patient has completed 12 total cycles of systemic therapy: cycles 1-8 with FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab (oxaliplatin discontinued after cycle 8 secondary to grade 3 cumulative peripheral sensory neuropathy), then cycles 9-12 with modified maintenance 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin plus bevacizumab. Prior sigmoid colectomy performed between cycles 6 and 9; no residual primary tumor. Restaging examination to assess response of hepatic metastatic disease. Comparison made to prior restaging CT performed after cycle 6 (approximately 3 months prior) and to the initial staging CT at time of diagnosis.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of iohexol intravenous contrast (portal venous phase, 70-second delay). Oral contrast was not administered. Axial images acquired at 3 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal reformats generated at 3 mm. Images reviewed in standard soft tissue, lung, and bone windows.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally without evidence of consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodules or masses are identified in either lung field. The airways are patent to the level of the subsegmental bronchi. No endobronchial lesions visualized.

Pleura: No pleural effusions. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Mediastinum and Hila: The cardiac silhouette is within normal limits for size. There is no pericardial effusion. The mediastinal contours are normal. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. The main pulmonary artery is not enlarged.

Chest Wall and Bones: A right-sided subcutaneous implanted venous access port (PORT-A-CATH) is again visualized with the catheter tip projecting at the cavoatrial junction, unchanged. No osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic disease are identified in the visualized ribs, clavicles, or thoracic spine.

Upper Abdomen -- LIVER:

The liver is normal in size with a smooth contour. No new hypodense lesions are identified. The previously characterized hepatic metastatic deposits show continued interval decrease in size compared to the initial staging examination and are overall stable to marginally decreased compared to the cycle 6 restaging CT:

-- Segment II lesion: A hypodense lesion in hepatic segment II measures approximately 0.7 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 0.9 cm at cycle 6 restaging, 1.4 cm at baseline staging). This lesion has become nearly imperceptible on the current study, appearing as a faint, poorly marginated hypodense focus. Residual enhancement is minimal.

-- Segment IVa lesion: The hypodense lesion in hepatic segment IVa measures approximately 1.1 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 1.4 cm at cycle 6 restaging, 2.1 cm at baseline). The lesion has well-defined margins and demonstrates no peripheral enhancement pattern. Continued size reduction is appreciated.

-- Segment VI lesion: A hypodense lesion in the posterior right lobe, hepatic segment VI, measures approximately 0.9 cm (previously 1.1 cm at cycle 6 restaging, 1.7 cm at baseline). This lesion remains hypodense relative to surrounding hepatic parenchyma without evidence of internal necrosis or hemorrhage.

-- Segment VII lesion (dominant): The dominant hepatic metastasis in segment VII measures approximately 1.8 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 2.1 cm at cycle 6 restaging, 3.2 cm at baseline staging). This lesion remains the largest of the hepatic deposits and demonstrates a rounded, hypoattenuating appearance with well-circumscribed margins. There is no invasion of the adjacent hepatic veins or right posterior biliary radicles, though the lesion abuts the right hepatic vein without definitive encasement. No satellite nodules are identified in the immediate vicinity.

No new hypodense hepatic lesions are identified. The portal and hepatic veins are patent throughout. The intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is unremarkable with no cholelithiasis or wall thickening.

SPLEEN: The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

PANCREAS: The pancreas is normal in size, contour, and attenuation without evidence of a focal mass, ductal dilatation, or peripancreatic stranding.

ADRENAL GLANDS: Both adrenal glands appear normal. No adrenal nodules or masses.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS: Both kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric nephrographic enhancement. There are bilateral small cortical cysts (largest 1.2 cm, right kidney), simple in appearance and unchanged from prior studies. No hydronephrosis or hydroureter.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT:

The stomach is unremarkable. Small bowel loops demonstrate no evidence of obstruction, wall thickening, or mesenteric edema.

Colon/Rectum: Post-surgical changes are noted in the left lower quadrant consistent with prior laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy and primary anastomosis. There is expected post-operative soft tissue thickening and a small amount of pericolic fat stranding at the anastomotic site. Importantly, no discrete soft tissue mass, luminal narrowing, or pathologically enlarged adjacent lymph nodes are identified at or near the anastomotic region. The descending colon proximal to the anastomosis and the rectum distal to the anastomosis are unremarkable. No intraluminal lesions are identified.

MESENTERY AND PERITONEUM: The mesenteric fat is preserved without evidence of nodular soft tissue deposits, omental caking, or nodular peritoneal thickening. No ascites is identified. No peritoneal implants are visualized. The previously noted mesenteric lymph nodes in the distribution of the prior sigmoid primary are no longer prominently enlarged.

LYMPH NODES -- ABDOMEN AND PELVIS: No pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal, mesenteric, iliac, or inguinal lymph nodes are identified. Several sub-centimeter lymph nodes are noted in the upper aorto-caval region and the right iliac chain, all measuring less than 8 mm in short axis and not meeting CT size criteria for pathologic enlargement.

PELVIS: The pelvic organs are unremarkable. Urinary bladder appears normal without wall thickening or intraluminal lesion. No pelvic free fluid.

BONES (VISUALIZED): No osteolytic or osteosclerotic lesions suspicious for osseous metastatic disease are identified in the visualized spine, pelvis, sacrum, or proximal femora.

SOFT TISSUES: No significant subcutaneous or deep soft tissue abnormality beyond the expected post-operative changes from prior port placement and laparoscopic colectomy.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued favorable response of bilobar hepatic metastatic disease compared to baseline. All four previously identified hepatic deposits -- in segments II, IVa, VI, and VII -- have demonstrated substantial and progressive size reduction since baseline staging CT. Aggregate reduction in hepatic metastatic burden is marked and sustained, with the dominant segment VII lesion now measuring 1.8 cm compared to 3.2 cm at initial presentation. The segment II lesion has nearly resolved on the current study. Overall, the hepatic metastatic disease shows maintained and deepened response to the systemic therapy regimen.

2. No new hepatic lesions identified.

3. The lungs remain clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules or masses are identified. No evidence of pulmonary metastatic disease on this examination.

4. Post-surgical changes at the sigmoid colectomy anastomotic site are stable and expected. No imaging evidence of locoregional tumor recurrence at the anastomosis.

5. No peritoneal carcinomatosis, ascites, or pathologically enlarged lymph nodes.

6. Right chest wall PORT-A-CATH with catheter tip at the cavoatrial junction, unchanged.

7. Bilateral simple renal cortical cysts, stable and benign in appearance.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with a well-maintained and substantive treatment response to the current systemic chemotherapy regimen, with continued reduction of hepatic metastatic disease relative to baseline and no development of new sites of disease at this time.
